feature.name: Controlled Frame API (available only to IWAs)

intake.finding: ChromeStatus entry created 2023-01-25 by Chase Phillips (cmp@google.com) as an Isolated Web Apps-specific API. The motivation is concrete and well-scoped: IWAs need to embed and control arbitrary third-party content that <iframe> cannot (blocked by X-Frame-Options/CSP), for virtual-desktop media overlays, kiosks, and managed browsing sessions. The problem is tightly coupled to the Chrome Apps deprecation timeline (kiosk Chrome Apps reach end of life at ChromeOS 150, full EOL ChromeOS 168/Feb 2028), making Controlled Frame the designated IWA successor to the Chrome Apps <webview> API. Initial public proposal (WICG/proposals#88) and explainer followed within days. However, adoption_expectation, availability_expectation, and adoption_plan were left empty, so no measurable success criteria were set at intake.
intake.limitations: No documented adoption/availability targets in the ChromeStatus record to later validate against.

incubation.finding: Incubated in WICG with an explainer (last updated 2023-01-30) and a work-in-progress spec at wicg.github.io/controlled-frame. The explainer explicitly scopes the API to IWAs and admits a known implementation limitation (a policy-controlled feature may be visible to normal web pages via script-based policy introspection, 'not intentional'). Multi-stakeholder grounding is structurally weak: the underlying IWA proposal received a negative Mozilla standards position and no WebKit response, so the embedding API inherits that posture. The explainer has not been materially updated since Jan 2023 despite a 2026 ship, leaving security/design assumptions unrefreshed.
incubation.limitations: Explainer revision history not enumerated; only the top-level 'last updated' date was observable.

prototype.finding: Intent to Prototype was posted to blink-dev (Jan 2023, corroborated by the @intenttoship mirror and referenced from the Ready-for-Trial thread). Prototyping is explicitly modeled on the mature Chrome Apps WebView API and reuses its method/event surface (back/forward/reload, executeScript, insertCSS, WebRequest interception, contextMenus, clearData). Implementation lives in //chrome (requires embedder support) behind the IwaControlledFrame flag and Blink>ControlledFrame component, tracked under crbug 40191772 (originally 1233993).
prototype.limitations: Direct ITP thread body was not fully readable (Google Groups message-id URLs were rejected by the reader); prototype existence is corroborated via the Ready-for-Trial thread's back-reference and the X mirror.

developerTrials.finding: A Ready-for-Trial / developer trial was declared (estimated milestone M114, desktop_first=114) with a public test_app and devtrial instructions. At trial time TAG review was explicitly 'Not yet requested'. The activation_risk field honestly flags that building+deploying an IWA via managed enterprise distribution is a significant activation hurdle. Observable feedback volume is thin: the controlled-frame repo has only ~38 stars/11 forks, and one open issue ('is the API available in kiosk ChromeOS apps?') signals availability confusion. No origin trial was ever run (origintrial=false; stage 150 goals/risks empty).
developerTrials.limitations: No public dev-trial feedback digest; partner feedback referenced only generically ('internal partners have requested embedding APIs').

wideReview.finding: Cross-ecosystem validation effectively did not occur. A W3C TAG review was requested only in March 2025 (#1067), roughly two years after incubation and immediately before ship; it concedes that without the IWA context 'we wouldn't recommend supporting an API like this' and that Mozilla's position on IWA was negative and WebKit non-responsive. ChromeStatus records Firefox/WebKit 'No signal' and Web developers 'No signals'. This is partly structural: Chromium's own IWA launch guidance states TAG review should NOT be requested for IWA-specific APIs and that Mozilla/Apple are 'disinterested', and recommends soliciting JS-platform communities (Electron/Node/Deno/Bun) and WinterTC instead — but no evidence of such outreach was found. The late, governance-contradicting TAG request yielded no external endorsement.
wideReview.limitations: No public record of Electron/WinterTC outreach was located, so absence is not proof of non-attempt; however the ChromeStatus standards-position fields and TAG thread show no cross-engine support.

experiment.finding: No formal experiment existed: no origin trial (origintrial=false, no OT id, stage 150 empty), no Finch field trial, and no public field data. Experimentation was limited to the long-running behind-a-flag developer trial (M114→M140). ChromeStatus lists internal partner interest but provides no partner names, metrics, or telemetry.
experiment.limitations: Private/enterprise partner trials, if any, are not publicly observable; no experiment artifacts to evaluate.

prepareToShip.finding: Intent to Ship thread exists (stage 160, intent_stage 5, desktop_first=140) and the IWA launch process requires it to be sent manually to blink-dev + iwa-dev@chromium.org with IWA-owner approval and 3 Blink API-owner LGTMs. Security review and Privacy review are both marked 'Issues addressed'. Developer documentation was finalized on developer.chrome.com (Controlled Frame doc, last updated 2025-09-18). However, adoption_expectation/availability_expectation remain empty at ship, and the Intent to Ship body itself could not be read directly (Google Groups message-id URL rejected by the reader), so LGTM/IWA-owner approval is only indirectly evidenced.
prepareToShip.limitations: Could not confirm the actual LGTM count or IWA-owner sign-off from the ITS thread body.

release.finding: Shipped enabled-by-default on desktop in Chrome 140 (stage 160 desktop_first=140; is_released=true; ChromeStatus search surface shows 'Shipping: 140'). Not on Android/iOS/WebView (all_platforms=false; conceptually a ChromeOS/desktop WebView analogue). Timing is strategically aligned with Chrome Apps kiosk deprecation (kiosk disabled-by-default from ChromeOS 138). However, practical availability is severely constrained: the API is IWA-only, requires //chrome embedder support, the ChromeStatus status field still reads 'In developer trial (Behind a flag)' (a data-quality anomaly vs. the ship milestone), IWAs are enterprise-managed and were ChromeOS-only at ship, and a Google-managed IWA allowlist gate was added in Chrome 143. So 'enabled by default' denotes code shipped in the build, not broad GA availability.
release.limitations: Exact stable-channel date of Chrome 140 not pinned; the 'behind a flag' vs 'shipped' status contradiction in ChromeStatus could not be reconciled from primary sources.

adoption.finding: An exact-scope use counter (HTMLControlledFrameElement, bucket 5624; mapping confidence 'exact', not a WebDX family/property counter) is now cached and shows near-zero, declining public page-load usage across its observation window (first point 2025-08-14; 337 daily points through 2026-07-21): peak 0.000023% on 2025-08-15, first observed positive 0.000007% on 2025-08-14, 0.000001% at Chrome 140 stable (2025-09-02), then 0% at day30 (2025-10-02), 0.000001% at day90 (2025-12-01), 0% at day180 (2026-03-01), and ~0.0000002% (day_percentage 2.2e-7) by 2026-07-21. These values are the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on which the counter fired — not unique developers, sites, users, successful tasks, or production deployment; the counter only begins 2025-08-14, so any earlier dev-trial usage is not captured by this exact property. Non-usage signals remain weak: no independent production case study (searches returned only generic kiosk/signage vendors, none referencing Controlled Frame), low explainer-repo traction (~38 stars, 11 forks, 3 open issues including a 2026 message-passing request and a 2025 availability-confusion question), and the IWA platform still self-described as 'currently under development' in Chrome Enterprise documentation. Because no adoption target was declared at intake/ship and there is no production/retention corroboration, the measured level/trend is described rather than scored; near-zero public telemetry is consistent with an enterprise-gated, IWA-only, allowlisted API and is not proof of zero real-world use.
adoption.limitations: Exact counter underrepresents enterprise/IWA and opt-out populations in UMA; no unique-site, production-deployment, retention, task-success, or user-benefit breakdown; no predeclared adoption target. Private enterprise deployments remain unobservable.

support.finding: Insufficient public evidence to score support burden or health. Tracking bug 40191772 remains open; the repo has 3 open issues (including availability confusion and host/guest message-passing). High-quality docs exist and were maintained through 2025-09-18. The Chrome 143 IWA allowlist adds deployment friction for support. No regression storm or significant issue volume is observable, but the feature is too new/niche for a defensible support-health read.
support.limitations: Crash/bug statistics and internal support volume are not public.

deprecation.finding: Controlled Frame itself is newly shipped (Chrome 140); no deprecation is planned or applicable for it within this review window. The relevant deprecation is of its predecessor (Chrome Apps <webview>), tracked separately (ChromeOS kiosk EOL at 150; full EOL 168/Feb 2028), and is treated as motivation context rather than an outcome of this feature.
deprecation.limitations: None; phase replayed and assessed as not-relevant by rationale, not as untested.

developerValue.finding: Strong problem-solution fit for a narrow, high-trust audience: embedding arbitrary content (bypassing X-Frame-Options/CSP) plus deep control (script/CSS injection, request interception, custom context menus, storage partitioning) is a real, articulated need for kiosks, signage, classroom/managed sessions, and virtual-desktop overlays, and it directly fills the Chrome Apps WebView gap. Value is materially capped because it is reachable only through the IWA platform, which is enterprise-managed and was ChromeOS-only at ship.
developerValue.limitations: No quantitative developer-satisfaction or partner-count evidence; 'internal partners have requested' is the only adoption-need signal and is unsourced.

endUserImpact.finding: End users are impacted only indirectly (via kiosk/signage/managed-session experiences). The design deliberately privileges the embedding IWA/administrator over third-party content publishers by allowing override of their embedding opt-outs and script injection into their pages — a tradeoff the TAG review flagged and the IWA trust model is meant to justify. No measurable end-user-impact data (accessibility, privacy, security incidents) exists.
endUserImpact.limitations: No user-research artifacts surfaced; security/privacy questionnaires exist but their resolutions were not read in full.

adoption.finding: Measured adoption is near-zero and declining in public Chrome page-load telemetry: the exact HTMLControlledFrameElement counter (bucket 5624) peaked at 0.000023% on 2025-08-15, was 0.000001% at Chrome 140 stable (2025-09-02), and ~0.0000002% (2.2e-7) by 2026-07-21, with launch-relative checkpoints day30=0%, day90=0.000001%, day180=0% (337 daily points from 2025-08-14). This is the share of measured page loads where the counter fired — not unique developers/sites/users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit. Corroborating signals are absent or weak: no production case studies, low explainer-repo traction, active availability confusion in issues, and the host IWA platform still self-described as 'under development' and gated by a Google-managed allowlist from Chrome 143. No adoption target was declared, and there is no production/retention corroboration, so per method the outcome is unscored and the measured level/trend is described; near-zero public share is consistent with an enterprise-gated, IWA-only API but is not, by itself, a success or failure verdict.
adoption.limitations: Exact counter underrepresents enterprise/IWA traffic; no unique-site, production, retention, task-success, or user-benefit evidence; no predeclared target. Private enterprise deployments remain unobservable.

interoperability.finding: Single-browser, single-OS (desktop/ChromeOS; explicitly not Android), WPT:false with only Chromium-internal pseudo-WPT tests, and no cross-engine signal (Mozilla negative on IWA, WebKit no signal, webdev no signals). Chromium's IWA guidance concedes there is 'little value in requesting standards positions' for IWA APIs, so this foreclosure is partly by-design — but it still means there is no interoperability story and no path to Baseline.
interoperability.limitations: No web-features/Baseline or Web Platform Dashboard entry to consult; the API is not a candidate by construction.

implementationQuality.finding: Security and privacy reviews are marked 'Issues addressed'; the implementation reuses the battle-tested Chrome Apps WebView semantics and ships a pseudo-WPT test harness in //chrome/test/data/controlled_frame and controlled_frame_wpt_browsertest.cc. Negatives: no real web-platform-tests (interoperability gap), and the explainer itself documents a known policy-introspection leakage limitation for normal web pages that was still being investigated.
implementationQuality.limitations: No access to crash/regression telemetry or code-review history; quality assessment rests on review-status fields and the explainer's self-reported limitation.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Developer documentation is high quality and current (Controlled Frame doc updated 2025-09-18; IWA intro updated 2026-02-06) and accurately communicates the IWA-only constraints and the 'prefer iframe first' guidance. But the ChromeStatus record has data-quality inconsistencies: the status field reads 'In developer trial (Behind a flag)' at milestone 140 while the ship stage records desktop_first=140 and is_released=true; and adoption_expectation/availability_expectation/adoption_plan are all empty. The explainer is stale (last updated Jan 2023 for a 2026 ship).
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: Could not fully read the Intent to Ship thread body to confirm communications accuracy at ship.

support.finding: Too little public signal to score. Open tracking bug and 3 open explainer issues exist; docs are maintained; the Chrome 143 allowlist adds deployment friction. No defensible read on support burden or issue-resolution health is possible this early and at this niche.
support.limitations: Internal support volume and SLA data are not public.

overall.finding: Controlled Frame solves a real, well-scoped problem with a coherent, review-addressed security model and shipped on schedule aligned with Chrome Apps kiosk deprecation — a competent enterprise-scoped launch. But it is structurally single-vendor/single-OS with no interoperability path, its availability is heavily gated (IWA-only, enterprise-managed ChromeOS, embedder flag, allowlist from 143), and measured public-telemetry adoption is near-zero and declining (peak 0.000023%, latest ~0.0000002%) — described against an absent target rather than counted as success or failure. The ChromeStatus record also carries status/targets data-quality gaps. Per the method, overall must not hide these mixed-to-failing dimensions or convert an unscored adoption outcome into a binary verdict.
overall.limitations: Material evidence gaps (ITS thread body, private adoption, production/retention corroboration) remain; adoption is described, not scored, due to an absent target. See completion.

supports: Fugu tracking bug for Controlled Frame; open; migrated from original crbug 1233993

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status (use-counter timeline; bucket 5624 = HTMLControlledFrameElement)
supports: Exact-scope feature counter (HTMLControlledFrameElement; mapping confidence 'exact', not a WebDX family/property counter): 337 daily points from 2025-08-14 to 2026-07-21; first observed positive 0.000007% on 2025-08-14; peak 0.000023% on 2025-08-15; at Chrome 140 stable (2025-09-02) 0.000001%; day30 (2025-10-02) 0%; day90 (2025-12-01) 0.000001%; day180 (2026-03-01) 0%; latest (2026-07-21) ~0.0000002% (day_percentage 2.2e-7). Near-zero and declining share of measured page loads.
sources.limitations: Values are the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads on which the counter fired; not unique developers/sites/users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production deployment, or end-user benefit. Mapping is exact (HTMLControlledFrameElement), not a family/property counter. Counter first observed 2025-08-14, so pre-Aug-2025 dev-trial usage is not captured by this exact property; UMA underrepresents enterprise/opt-out populations; zero/missing points are not proof of zero real-world use; instrumentation may include probing. No predeclared adoption target exists to compare against. Timeline cached with SHA-256 7d9ccec62e53564e1a537e49704f378545ee32965815a359a889ed2d0442ffed.

successes: Clear, well-scoped problem (embed + control arbitrary content in a high-trust app) with a coherent security model that passed security and privacy review with 'Issues addressed'.

Strategic timing: shipped in Chrome 140 in lockstep with the Chrome Apps kiosk deprecation (disabled-by-default ChromeOS 138), directly filling the <webview> capability gap for kiosk/signage/managed-session apps.
Honest risk disclosure in ChromeStatus (activation_risks, interop_compat_risks, security_risks) and high-quality, current developer documentation with a working demo and IWA Kitchen Sink.
Reused the mature Chrome Apps WebView semantics, reducing design risk, and built a pseudo-WPT test harness given WPT cannot run in IWA contexts.
failures: No interoperability: single-browser, single-OS (no Android), WPT:false, Mozilla negative on the host IWA proposal, WebKit no signal, web developers 'no signals' — no Baseline path and no cross-engine owner.

No public production case studies and weak developer-engagement signals across a ~3-year availability window: low explainer-repo traction (~38 stars, 11 forks, 3 open issues) with active availability confusion ('is the API available in kiosk ChromeOS apps?'), and no production deployment surfaced by search. The exact use counter confirms near-zero public page-load usage (peak 0.000023% on 2025-08-15; latest ~0.0000002%), consistent with an enterprise-gated API — per method this measured level is described and the adoption outcome is left unscored rather than treated as a usage-number failure.

Severe practical-availability gating that makes 'enabled by default at 140' not equivalent to GA: IWA-only, enterprise-managed ChromeOS-only at ship, embedder flag, and a Google-managed allowlist added in Chrome 143.
Weak/late wide review: TAG review requested only in March 2025 and against Chromium's own IWA guidance (which says not to request it); no evidence of the recommended JS-platform/WinterTC outreach.
ChromeStatus data-quality gaps: status field reads 'In developer trial (Behind a flag)' while the ship stage records desktop_first=140, and adoption/availability expectations were left empty at ship.
Stale explainer (last updated Jan 2023) carrying an admitted policy-introspection leakage limitation still under investigation at a 2026 ship.
counterfactuals: If IWAs had been available beyond enterprise-managed ChromeOS (or if the Chrome 143 allowlist gate had not been introduced), activation friction would be materially lower and adoption might be observable; the platform-readiness gap is the dominant adoption constraint, not the API design.

If a cross-engine or JS-runtime owner (Electron/Node/Deno/Bun via WinterTC) had co-designed and adopted the surface, interoperability and a Baseline path could exist; the IWA-only scoping and Mozilla's negative IWA position foreclose this.

If adoption_expectation/availability_expectation had been defined at intake/ship, the now-available usage telemetry (near-zero public page-load share) could be judged against a target instead of merely described; their absence leaves the adoption outcome unscored.
If the explainer had been kept current and the policy-introspection leakage resolved before ship, security/wide-review assumptions could be validated against the shipped design.
If the TAG review had been requested early (not Mar 2025) and reconciled with the IWA guidance ('do not request TAG'), wide review would not have been a near-ship formality.
If the ChromeStatus status field had been reconciled with the ship stage, downstream tooling (and this retrospective) would not have to treat 'enabled by default' vs 'behind a flag' as contradictory.
skillImprovements: Add a constrained-availability-ship detection rule: when a feature has requires_embedder_support=true, is enterprise/IWA-scoped, or ships while the status field still reads 'Behind a flag', the report must explicitly distinguish 'code shipped/enabled by default' from 'broadly available/GA' and must not count the ship as adoption.

Add a ChromeStatus data-consistency check that flags when browsers.chrome.status text contradicts the ship stage / desktop milestone (e.g., status='In developer trial (Behind a flag)' vs stage 160 desktop_first=140 + is_released=true) and records which signal was treated as authoritative.

Add an IWA-specific-API phase packet: (a) verify the 'why IWA-specific' explainer section, (b) check that the manual Intent-to-Ship was sent to both blink-dev@ and iwa-dev@ and that IWA-owner approval + 3 Blink API-owner LGTMs are present, (c) treat interoperability/Baseline as 'not-relevant with rationale' (per Chromium IWA guidance) rather than as a failure to test, and (d) seek JS-platform/WinterTC and Electron signals instead of Mozilla/WebKit/TAG positions.

Add a prepareToShip adoption-targets gate: if adoption_expectation, availability_expectation, and adoption_plan are all empty at Intent-to-Ship, mark the transition packet incomplete and require explicit targets (or a documented rationale for their absence).

Add a successor-to-deprecated-feature linkage rule: when a feature is the designated successor to a deprecated API (here Chrome Apps <webview>), include the deprecation timeline as required context and check migration readiness — flag when successor-platform readiness (IWA enterprise/ChromeOS-only, allowlist) lags the predecessor's EOL milestones (ChromeOS kiosk EOL at 150).

Add a usage-data caching step: when a chromestatus.com use-counter popularity page is JS-rendered, fetch and cache the underlying timeline data endpoint (chromestatus.com/data/timeline/featurepopularity?bucket_id=...) with a SHA-256, record the mapping status (exact vs WebDX family/property vs origin-trial — a family/property/trial counter is not exact feature adoption), and capture launch-relative checkpoints (atStable/day30/day90/day180) plus first-observed/peak/latest, so near-zero or enterprise-scoped counters are reported with exact percentages rather than recorded as missing evidence.

missingEvidence: No unique-site, production-deployment, retention, task-success, or user-benefit breakdown: the exact HTMLControlledFrameElement use counter (S22) reports only the share of measured page loads where the counter fired, not who uses it in production or whether tasks succeed. Private enterprise deployments are unobservable.

No predeclared adoption/availability target at intake or ship (adoption_expectation/availability_expectation/adoption_plan all empty), so the near-zero measured telemetry can only be described, not judged against a threshold.

Intent to Ship thread body (Google Groups message-id URLs were rejected by the reader): LGTM count, IWA-owner sign-off, and ship comms could not be read directly, only inferred from ChromeStatus fields and the IWA governance doc.
No public adoption/partner case studies: searches found no production deployment of Controlled Frame; partner interest is referenced only generically ('internal partners have requested').
Timed-out reads: chromeos.dev 'PWAs: Past, Present, Future' post and the blink-dev IWA-allowlist PSA page did not load fully (content partially recovered via search snippets + secondary docs).
TAG review eventual position/comments beyond the opening request were not read; security/privacy questionnaire resolutions were not read in full.
Internal Chromium crash/regression telemetry and support volume are not public, leaving support-health unscored.
